skill.name: lark-okr
skill.description: Use Lark OKR commands to view cycles, inspect objectives and key results, and manage OKR alignment and metrics.
skill.summary: Manage Lark OKR cycles, objectives, key results, alignments, and progress metrics through lark-cli.
skill.author: larksuite

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, filesystem, URL, and key-related hits, but review found these are mostly Markdown command examples, relative documentation links, sample URLs, and OKR field names. The real risk is operational: the skill instructs use of lark-cli commands that can read, create, update, or delete Lark OKR data when the user has credentials and scopes.

medium_findings.title: Lark CLI Commands Can Modify OKR Data
medium_findings.description: The skill requires lark-cli and documents commands for OKR APIs, including create, patch, delete, position, and weight operations. This is legitimate for an OKR management skill, but it can change or delete business data if used with write scopes.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The CLI requirement and write/delete scopes are explicit in the documentation. The behavior is intended rather than malicious, so the finding is medium operational risk.

medium_findings.title: Networked Lark API Access Requires Scoped Credentials
medium_findings.description: The skill is built for the Lark OKR API and lists read and write scopes for OKR content, periods, and settings. Users must ensure credentials are scoped to the requested task.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The API path and scope table show authenticated network access. No exfiltration intent was found, but broad scopes can expose or modify OKR data.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Hits Are Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: The analyzer flagged many backticks and bash fences as Ruby or shell execution. The reviewed lines are documentation examples and inline command names, not executable code in the skill package.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged content is inside Markdown code fences or inline formatting. No script file, dynamic execution API, or user-input command construction was found.

low_findings.title: Relative Documentation Links Flagged As Path Traversal
low_findings.description: The path traversal hits are relative Markdown links to shared Lark documentation and local reference pages. They do not perform filesystem reads or writes by themselves.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are Markdown links such as sibling documentation references. No filesystem API or traversal of user-supplied paths appears in the skill files.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Sample Rich Text Values
low_findings.description: The URL hits are examples inside the OKR ContentBlock documentation. They show link and image fields and do not initiate network requests.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear only inside JSON documentation examples. There is no code path that fetches or posts to these URLs.

low_findings.title: Sensitive Key Findings Are OKR Key Result Terms
low_findings.description: The key-related hits refer to OKR key result API names and fields. No certificate, private key, token, or secret material was found in the reviewed lines.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The term key appears as part of key_results, which is a business object in OKR. The lines contain scope names and API names, not secret values.

content.value_statement: Teams need accurate OKR updates without guessing Lark API fields. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through lark-cli workflows for cycles, objectives, key results, alignments, and metrics.
actual_capabilities: Lists Lark OKR cycles for a specified user with optional time range filters.
Retrieves objectives and key results for a selected OKR cycle.
Explains OKR entities, fields, statuses, scopes, and relationships.
Documents ContentBlock formatting for objective, key result, and note fields.
Guides native lark-cli OKR API calls after checking schema requirements.
Identifies required Lark permission scopes for read and write operations.
limitations: Requires lark-cli, Lark authentication, and authorized OKR scopes.
Does not provide credentials or bypass tenant permissions.
Write operations can change business data and should be reviewed before execution.
Entity IDs must come from Lark or earlier commands; the skill cannot infer missing IDs.

use_cases.title: Review Team OKR Cycles
use_cases.description: Find active or historical OKR cycles for a user and summarize dates, status, and cycle IDs.

use_cases.title: Inspect Objective Progress
use_cases.description: Retrieve a cycle detail and summarize objectives, key results, scores, weights, and deadlines.

use_cases.title: Prepare Safe OKR Updates
use_cases.description: Check schema and permission requirements before creating, patching, deleting, or reordering OKR records.

prompt_templates.title: List My OKR Cycles
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Lark OKR skill to list my OKR cycles. Resolve my user ID first if needed, then summarize the active cycles.

prompt_templates.title: Inspect One Cycle
prompt_templates.prompt: Use cycle ID 1234567890123456789 to get the OKR cycle detail. Summarize each objective, key result, score, weight, and deadline.

prompt_templates.title: Plan an Objective Update
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to update an OKR objective. Check the lark-cli schema first, identify required fields and scopes, then propose the command for review.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Alignment and Metrics
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this cycle for objective alignments and indicator progress. Identify missing metrics, risky statuses, and safe lark-cli commands to correct them.

output_examples.input: List OKR cycles for a user in 2025 Q1 and Q2.
output_examples.output: The assistant reports matching cycles, date ranges, statuses, and cycle IDs. It notes which cycle should be used for detailed objective review.

output_examples.input: Show details for one OKR cycle.
output_examples.output: The assistant summarizes objectives, key results, owners, deadlines, scores, and weights in plain language.

output_examples.input: Prepare to update a key result progress metric.
output_examples.output: The assistant checks the schema, identifies the needed indicator fields, lists required scopes, and asks for confirmation before any write operation.

best_practices: Run schema checks before native API commands so field formats are not guessed.
Use dry-run or review planned commands before write or delete operations.
Confirm user IDs, cycle IDs, and permission scopes before retrieving or changing OKR data.
anti_patterns: Do not run write or delete commands without a clear user request and confirmation.
Do not guess ContentBlock JSON or OKR entity IDs from names alone.
Do not use broad tenant credentials when a user-scoped token is enough.

faq.question: What does this skill manage?
faq.answer: It manages Lark OKR cycles, objectives, key results, alignments, categories, and progress indicators through documented lark-cli commands.

faq.question: Does it require lark-cli?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill depends on lark-cli and valid Lark authentication before it can access OKR data.

faq.question: Can it edit OKRs?
faq.answer: Yes, if the authenticated account has write scopes. Review commands carefully before creating, patching, deleting, or reordering records.

faq.question: Can it find my user ID?
faq.answer: It can guide the assistant to resolve a user ID through related Lark contact commands when those tools and permissions are available.

faq.question: Does it expose secrets?
faq.answer: No evidence of embedded secrets was found. It still uses authenticated Lark access, so credentials should remain scoped and protected.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
